My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?

input.initialPrompt: My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?

data.content:
## Collect a **Thread Time** trace with PerfView

Thread Time profiling captures both CPU and **off-CPU waits** (locks, I/O, thread pool starvation), which is exactly what you need for diagnosing slow requests under load.

### PerfView command

```powershell
PerfView.exe collect /ThreadTime /NoGui /AcceptEULA /MaxCollectSec:30 /CircularMB:1024 /Zip:True /Merge:True
```

### Key arguments explained

| Argument | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `/ThreadTime` | Enables context-switch tracing — captures **blocked time**, not just CPU |
| `/CircularMB:1024` | 1 GB circular buffer; prevents disk from filling under load |
| `/MaxCollectSec:30` | Auto-stops after 30s; adjust to cover your repro window |
| `/Merge:True` | Resolves symbols into the `.etl` at collection time |
| `/Zip:True` | Produces a portable `.etl.zip` |

### Analysis workflow

1. Open the `.etl.zip` in PerfView.
2. Go to **Advanced Group → Thread Time Stacks**.
3. Filter to your process (`dotnet` or your app name).
4. Look at **BLOCKED_TIME** and **READYTHREAD** nodes — these show where threads were waiting (locks, async I/O, `Task.Wait`, `Monitor.Enter`, etc.).

> **Tip:** If you suspect thread pool starvation specifically, also add `/ClrEvents:ThreadPool` to capture thread pool work-item queuing events.
